title: Hello World first program
content: Hello World first program in Python is a tiny program that prints a simple message, often with print("Hello, world!"). Beginners use it to see that code can produce visible output on the screen. It is a good first step before learning variables, input, and decisions.
aliases: Hello World first program là gì

title: Python print output
content: Python print output in Python means showing text or values in the console with the print function. Beginners use it to check results, display messages, and understand what a program is doing. For beginners, print is also a simple debugging tool.

title: Python input function
content: Python input function in Python reads text typed by the user and returns it as a string. Beginners use it to make interactive console programs instead of fixed programs. If a number is needed, the text usually must be converted with int or float.

title: Variable assignment with equals
content: Variable assignment with equals in Python uses the equals sign to make a name refer to a value, such as score = 10. Beginners use it to store values that may be reused later in the program. This is assignment, not the same idea as mathematical equality in every context.
aliases: Variable assignment with equals là gì

title: Python variable naming rules
content: Python variable naming rules in Python allow letters, digits, and underscores, but the name should not start with a digit. Beginners use it to create readable names such as total_price or student_count. Good names help beginners understand code faster than short unclear names.
aliases: Python variable naming rules là gì

title: Python comments basic use
content: Python comments basic use in Python uses the hash symbol to write notes that Python ignores while running code. Beginners use it to explain why code exists or temporarily mark reminders. Comments should clarify intent, not repeat every obvious line.
aliases: Python comments basic use là gì

title: Python expression basic
content: Python expression basic in Python is a piece of code that produces a value, such as 2 + 3 or name.upper(). Beginners use it to build calculations, conditions, and function arguments. Understanding expressions helps beginners read longer lines step by step.

title: Python statement basic
content: Python statement basic in Python is an instruction that Python can execute, such as assignment, import, or a function call. Beginners use it to make a program do work in a clear order. A program is usually a sequence of statements controlled by branches and loops.

title: Assignment versus equality
content: Assignment versus equality in Python separates changing a variable from testing two values for equality. Beginners use it to avoid confusing x = 5 with x == 5. In conditions, beginners usually need == to compare, not = to assign.

title: Python arithmetic operators
content: Python arithmetic operators in Python include addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, floor division, modulo, and exponentiation. Beginners use it to write calculations such as totals, averages, and remainders. Operator precedence can affect results, so parentheses are useful for clarity.

title: Modulo operator remainder
content: Modulo operator remainder in Python uses the percent sign to return a remainder after division. Beginners use it to check even numbers, cycle through positions, or wrap values around. For example, n % 2 == 0 is a common even-number test.

title: Floor division operator
content: Floor division operator in Python uses double slash to divide and round down toward a whole-number result. Beginners use it to get a quotient without the fractional part. It is different from normal division, which returns a floating-point result in Python 3.

title: Operator precedence basic
content: Operator precedence basic in Python means Python evaluates some operations before others, such as multiplication before addition. Beginners use it to predict calculation results and write clearer formulas. When in doubt, beginners should add parentheses instead of relying on memory.

title: String concatenation basic
content: String concatenation basic in Python means joining strings together with the plus operator. Beginners use it to build simple messages from smaller text values. It only works directly with strings, so numbers often need conversion first.

title: Python f-string basic
content: Python f-string basic in Python is a readable way to put values inside text using braces inside a string. Beginners use it to format output such as names, scores, and totals. F-strings are often clearer than repeated string concatenation for beginner programs.

title: User input is a string
content: User input is a string in Python means input returns text even when the user types digits. Beginners use it to avoid surprise TypeError problems in calculations. Use int or float conversion when the program needs numeric arithmetic.
aliases: User input is a string là gì

title: Simple calculator input pattern
content: Simple calculator input pattern in Python usually combines input, type conversion, arithmetic operators, and print output. Beginners use it to practice the full flow of reading, computing, and displaying a result. The common beginner mistake is forgetting to convert input text into numbers.
aliases: Simple calculator input pattern là gì

title: Python indentation blocks
content: Python indentation blocks in Python uses indentation to group code inside if statements, loops, functions, and classes. Beginners use it to show which statements belong together. Wrong indentation can change program logic or cause an error.

title: Python names are case sensitive
content: Python names are case sensitive in Python treats Name, name, and NAME as different identifiers. Beginners use it to avoid bugs caused by inconsistent spelling or capitalization. Beginners should choose one clear naming style and use it consistently.
aliases: Python names are case sensitive là gì

title: Python reserved keywords
content: Python reserved keywords in Python are words with special meaning, such as if, for, while, def, and class. Beginners use it to understand why some words cannot be used as variable names. Choosing descriptive non-keyword names prevents confusing syntax errors.

title: Python import statement
content: Python import statement in Python loads code from a module so a program can use extra functions or objects. Beginners use it to reuse standard library tools instead of writing everything from zero. The module must exist in the environment where the program runs.

title: Python standard library beginner use
content: Python standard library beginner use in Python is the collection of modules included with Python for common tasks. Beginners use it to use tools for math, dates, randomness, files, and more. Beginners should learn a few useful modules before installing many external packages.
aliases: Python standard library beginner use là gì

title: Running a Python script
content: Running a Python script in Python means saving code in a .py file and executing it with Python. Beginners use it to test a complete program outside the interactive prompt. The terminal must run in the correct folder or use the correct file path.
aliases: Running a Python script là gì

title: Python interactive mode
content: Python interactive mode in Python lets a learner type one expression or statement at a time and see results immediately. Beginners use it to experiment with small ideas before writing a full script. It is helpful for learning but not a replacement for saving real programs.

title: Readable variable names
content: Readable variable names in Python make code easier to understand by describing the value's purpose. Beginners use it to communicate intent to the future reader of the program. Names like total_score are usually better than a, b, or temp for beginner code.

title: Pseudocode for planning
content: Pseudocode for planning in Python is informal step-by-step writing before real code. Beginners use it to plan the logic of a program without worrying about syntax first. It can help beginners solve the problem before fighting Python errors.

title: Console program basic
content: Console program basic in Python runs through text input and output in a terminal or command window. Beginners use it to practice programming logic without building a graphical interface. Many beginner exercises use console programs because they are simple to test.

title: Python code order top down
content: Python code order top down in Python usually runs statements from top to bottom unless control flow changes the path. Beginners use it to predict what happens first, next, and last. Functions and classes may be defined before they are called later.
aliases: Python code order top down là gì

title: Literal value basic
content: Literal value basic in Python is a value written directly in code, such as 10, 3.14, "cat", or True. Beginners use it to understand examples before variables are introduced. Literals are useful, but repeated raw values can make larger programs harder to maintain.

title: Beginner program structure
content: Beginner program structure in Python often follows input, processing, and output as a simple pattern. Beginners use it to organize small programs like calculators, converters, and quizzes. Keeping these parts clear makes debugging easier for new learners.

title: Python integer type
content: Python integer type in Python represents whole numbers without a fractional part. Beginners use it to count items, store ages, and work with exact whole-number values. Integers can be very large in Python, but they still must be used with the correct operations.

title: Python float type
content: Python float type in Python represents numbers with decimal points using floating-point arithmetic. Beginners use it to handle measurements, division results, and approximate real-number calculations. Floating-point values may have tiny rounding errors, so exact equality can surprise beginners.

title: Floating-point precision warning
content: Floating-point precision warning in Python means decimal values may not be stored exactly inside the computer. Beginners use it to understand why 0.1 + 0.2 may display a tiny error. For money or exact decimal work, special approaches may be needed.

title: Python string type
content: Python string type in Python represents text as a sequence of characters. Beginners use it to store names, messages, file lines, and user input. Strings are immutable, so operations usually create a new string instead of changing the old one.

title: Python boolean type
content: Python boolean type in Python has two main truth values, True and False. Beginners use it to control decisions, loop conditions, and validation checks. Boolean values often come from comparisons like score >= 50.

title: Python None value
content: Python None value in Python represents the absence of a normal value. Beginners use it to mark missing results, default states, or functions that do not return useful data. It is not the same as zero, an empty string, or False.

title: Python type function
content: Python type function in Python shows the type of a value, such as int, str, list, or dict. Beginners use it to inspect data while learning or debugging. It helps beginners confirm what input or conversion produced.

title: Python int conversion
content: Python int conversion in Python tries to convert a value into an integer. Beginners use it to turn numeric text from input into a number for arithmetic. It raises an error when the text is not a valid whole number.

title: Python float conversion
content: Python float conversion in Python tries to convert a value into a floating-point number. Beginners use it to read decimal input such as height, price, or temperature. It can still fail if the input contains letters or invalid formatting.

title: Python str conversion
content: Python str conversion in Python turns a value into text. Beginners use it to combine numbers with messages or prepare output. It does not change the original value unless the result is stored.

title: Input to integer conversion
content: Input to integer conversion in Python is needed because input returns text by default. Beginners use it to use typed digits in calculations like age + 1. A beginner should handle invalid input when users may type non-numeric text.
aliases: Input to integer conversion là gì

title: ValueError from conversion
content: ValueError from conversion in Python often happens when int or float cannot understand the given text. Beginners use it to find mistakes in user input or file data. The fix is usually to validate the input or catch the exception.

title: Boolean conversion caution
content: Boolean conversion caution in Python can make many values truthy or falsy rather than simply reading words as true or false. Beginners use it to write conditions based on actual Python behavior. For example, bool("False") is True because the string is not empty.

title: Comparison result is boolean
content: Comparison result is boolean in Python means expressions like a < b produce True or False. Beginners use it to build if conditions and loop checks. This helps beginners see that comparisons are values, not just questions.
aliases: Comparison result is boolean là gì

title: Mutable and immutable values
content: Mutable and immutable values in Python separates objects that can be changed from objects that cannot be changed in place. Beginners use it to understand why lists behave differently from strings and tuples. This idea matters when passing data into functions.
aliases: Mutable and immutable values là gì

title: String immutability
content: String immutability in Python means an existing string cannot be changed character by character. Beginners use it to avoid expecting name[0] = "A" to work. String methods return new strings, so the result should be stored if needed.

title: List mutability
content: List mutability in Python means a list can be changed after it is created. Beginners use it to append, remove, sort, or update items in a collection. Mutability is useful but can cause aliasing bugs when two names refer to the same list.

title: Tuple immutability
content: Tuple immutability in Python means the tuple structure cannot be changed after creation. Beginners use it to store fixed groups of values such as coordinates or return pairs. A tuple can still contain mutable objects, so beginners should read examples carefully.

title: Truthy and falsy values
content: Truthy and falsy values in Python means Python can treat certain values as true or false in conditions. Beginners use it to write shorter checks for empty strings, empty lists, zero, or None. Clear comparisons may be better for beginners when logic is confusing.
aliases: Truthy and falsy values là gì

title: Empty collections are falsy
content: Empty collections are falsy in Python means empty lists, dictionaries, sets, and tuples act like False in conditions. Beginners use it to check whether a collection has any data. This is common Python style, but explicit len checks can be clearer at first.
aliases: Empty collections are falsy là gì

title: is versus equals
content: is versus equals in Python separates object identity from value equality. Beginners use it to avoid using is when == is needed to compare normal values. Use is mainly for identity checks such as value is None.

title: Python dynamic typing
content: Python dynamic typing in Python means variable names can refer to values of different types during program execution. Beginners use it to write flexible code without declaring variable types first. Beginners still need to track types mentally to avoid wrong operations.

title: Python type annotation hint
content: Python type annotation hint in Python is an optional note about the expected type of a variable, parameter, or return value. Beginners use it to make code easier to read and check with tools. Annotations do not automatically force runtime type safety in normal Python.
aliases: Python type annotation hint là gì

title: Python round function caution
content: Python round function caution in Python rounds a number for display or approximate calculation. Beginners use it to format decimal output in beginner programs. Floating-point behavior can still surprise learners, so rounded output is not always exact storage.
aliases: Python round function caution là gì

title: Python abs function
content: Python abs function in Python returns the non-negative magnitude of a number. Beginners use it to calculate distances from zero or compare differences. It is simple but useful in math exercises and error measurements.

title: Python len function
content: Python len function in Python returns the number of items in a sequence or collection. Beginners use it to check string length, list size, or dictionary entries. It is often used with loops, validation, and beginner statistics tasks.

title: Python strip method
content: Python strip method in Python removes whitespace from the start and end of a string. Beginners use it to clean user input or file lines before comparison. It does not remove spaces inside the middle of the text.

title: Python split method
content: Python split method in Python breaks a string into a list of smaller strings using a separator. Beginners use it to process words, CSV-like text, or space-separated numbers. Beginners should remember the result is a list of strings.

title: Python if statement
content: Python if statement in Python runs a block of code only when a condition is true. Beginners use it to make decisions such as pass or fail, yes or no, and valid or invalid. The condition should produce a boolean-like result.

title: Python elif chain
content: Python elif chain in Python checks another condition when the earlier if condition was false. Beginners use it to handle several possible cases without writing many separate if statements. Order matters because Python stops after the first matching branch.

title: Python else default case
content: Python else default case in Python runs when none of the earlier conditions in the same chain matched. Beginners use it to provide a fallback result or error message. It should not have its own condition.
aliases: Python else default case là gì

title: Comparison operators basic
content: Comparison operators basic in Python compare values using symbols such as ==, !=, <, <=, >, and >=. Beginners use it to create conditions for decisions and loops. Beginners should not confuse == comparison with = assignment. A beginner answer should stay concrete, use small examples, and avoid confusing advanced details.

title: Logical and operator
content: Logical and operator in Python requires both conditions to be true. Beginners use it to combine checks such as age >= 18 and has_ticket. If the first condition is false, Python may not need to evaluate the second one.

title: Logical or operator
content: Logical or operator in Python requires at least one condition to be true. Beginners use it to accept multiple valid choices or alternative cases. Parentheses can make mixed and-or logic easier to read.

title: Logical not operator
content: Logical not operator in Python reverses a truth value. Beginners use it to write conditions such as not found or not is_empty. Too many not operators can make beginner logic hard to follow.

title: Nested if statement
content: Nested if statement in Python places one decision inside another decision. Beginners use it to represent logic where a second check only matters after the first check passes. Deep nesting can become hard to read, so simple structure is preferred.

title: Condition order caution
content: Condition order caution in Python means earlier branches in an if-elif chain can prevent later branches from running. Beginners use it to avoid unreachable or wrong cases. Put more specific checks before broader checks when both could match.

title: Boolean flag variable
content: Boolean flag variable in Python stores True or False to remember a state such as found, valid, or running. Beginners use it to control loops and decisions more clearly. A good flag name should sound like a yes-or-no question.

title: For loop over iterable
content: For loop over iterable in Python repeats code for each item in a sequence or collection. Beginners use it to process every number, word, row, or object in a group. It is usually simpler than a while loop when the number of items is known.
aliases: For loop over iterable là gì

title: For loop with range
content: For loop with range in Python uses range to generate a sequence of numbers for repeated steps. Beginners use it to repeat code a fixed number of times or loop through indexes. Remember that range often stops before the final number.
aliases: For loop with range là gì

title: Range stop is exclusive
content: Range stop is exclusive in Python means range(5) gives 0 through 4, not 0 through 5. Beginners use it to avoid off-by-one mistakes in loops. This rule is common in Python indexing and beginner exercises.
aliases: Range stop is exclusive là gì

title: Range start stop step
content: Range start stop step in Python lets a loop choose a start number, stop boundary, and step size. Beginners use it to count upward, skip values, or count backward. The stop value is still excluded, even with a custom step.
aliases: Range start stop step là gì

title: For loop index caution
content: For loop index caution in Python means indexes are sometimes useful but not always needed. Beginners use it to avoid writing complicated index code when direct item iteration is enough. Use enumerate when both the index and item are needed.
aliases: For loop index caution là gì

title: Python while loop
content: Python while loop in Python repeats code while a condition remains true. Beginners use it to continue until input is valid, a game ends, or a counter reaches a limit. The loop must have a clear way to stop.

title: While loop infinite warning
content: While loop infinite warning in Python can happen when the loop condition never becomes false. Beginners use it to recognize why a program appears stuck. Update a counter, change state, or use a clear stopping condition.
aliases: While loop infinite warning là gì

title: Counter update in loop
content: Counter update in loop in Python changes a counter value during repeated execution. Beginners use it to count attempts, positions, or repeated actions. Forgetting the update is a common reason a while loop never stops.
aliases: Counter update in loop là gì

title: Python break statement
content: Python break statement in Python exits the nearest loop immediately. Beginners use it to stop searching after a result is found or leave a menu loop. It should be used carefully so the loop logic remains easy to understand.

title: Python continue statement
content: Python continue statement in Python skips the rest of the current loop iteration and moves to the next one. Beginners use it to ignore invalid or unwanted items without stopping the whole loop. Too many continue statements can make control flow harder to trace.

title: Python loop else basic
content: Python loop else basic in Python runs an else block after a loop finishes normally without break. Beginners use it to handle search failure in a clean way. This feature is useful but may confuse beginners, so explain it with small examples.
aliases: Python loop else basic là gì

title: Nested loops basic
content: Nested loops basic in Python use one loop inside another loop. Beginners use it to process tables, grids, pairs, or matrices. Nested loops can grow runtime quickly, so beginners should understand what each level repeats.

title: Accumulator pattern
content: Accumulator pattern in Python uses a variable that is updated repeatedly inside a loop. Beginners use it to build totals, products, joined text, or collected results. Initialize the accumulator before the loop and update it in the correct place.

title: Counting matches in a loop
content: Counting matches in a loop in Python uses a counter that increases when an item meets a condition. Beginners use it to count vowels, passing scores, or matching words. The condition and update must be inside the loop body.
aliases: Counting matches in a loop là gì

title: Menu loop basic
content: Menu loop basic in Python keeps showing choices until the user selects an exit option. Beginners use it to build small console programs with repeated actions. A clear exit condition prevents the menu from becoming an accidental infinite loop.

title: Sentinel value loop
content: Sentinel value loop in Python uses a special input value to stop repeated reading. Beginners use it to accept many numbers or names until the user types a stop word. The sentinel should be documented so users know how to finish.

title: Input validation loop
content: Input validation loop in Python repeats until the user gives acceptable input. Beginners use it to prevent invalid values from breaking later calculations. The loop should explain what went wrong and ask again clearly.

title: Off-by-one range error
content: Off-by-one range error in Python happens when a loop runs one time too many or too few. Beginners use it to debug indexing, counting, and boundary conditions. Checking the first and last loop values usually reveals the mistake.

title: Membership in operator
content: Membership in operator in Python checks whether a value appears in a collection or string. Beginners use it to write readable conditions like name in students. It works with many containers, but speed depends on the container type.

title: Python enumerate basic
content: Python enumerate basic in Python gives both an index and an item while looping. Beginners use it to avoid manually updating a separate index counter. It is useful when output should show item numbers or positions.

title: Function definition with def
content: Function definition with def in Python creates a reusable named block of code with the def keyword. Beginners use it to organize repeated behavior into one place. The function body must be indented under the definition line.

title: Function call basic
content: Function call basic in Python runs a function by writing its name followed by parentheses. Beginners use it to execute reusable logic whenever it is needed. Defining a function does not run it automatically.

title: Function parameter basic
content: Function parameter basic in Python is a name in a function definition that receives a value. Beginners use it to make a function work with different inputs. Good parameter names make the function easier to understand.

title: Function argument basic
content: Function argument basic in Python is the actual value passed into a function call. Beginners use it to supply data for the function to use. Beginners often mix up parameter names in definitions and argument values in calls.

title: Function return value
content: Function return value in Python sends a result back to the caller. Beginners use it to use the output in another calculation or condition. A function without an explicit return gives None.

title: Return versus print
content: Return versus print in Python separates giving a value back from displaying text on the screen. Beginners use it to write functions that can be reused in many contexts. Printing is visible output, while return is program data.

title: Function with no return gives None
content: Function with no return gives None in Python means Python returns None when a function finishes without a return statement. Beginners use it to debug unexpected None values in beginner programs. Use return when the caller needs a result.

title: Default parameter basic
content: Default parameter basic in Python provides a value used when the caller does not pass an argument. Beginners use it to make simple options without requiring every argument. Avoid mutable default values like empty lists unless you know the consequences.

title: Keyword argument basic
content: Keyword argument basic in Python passes a value by naming the parameter in the call. Beginners use it to make function calls clearer when there are several inputs. The keyword must match a parameter name in the function definition.

title: Positional argument basic
content: Positional argument basic in Python passes values based on their order in the function call. Beginners use it to write short calls when the meaning is obvious. Wrong order can cause subtle bugs if values have compatible types.

title: Local scope basic
content: Local scope basic in Python means a variable created inside a function normally belongs to that function. Beginners use it to prevent names inside functions from accidentally changing outside code. Trying to use a local variable outside the function causes confusion.

title: Global variable caution
content: Global variable caution in Python means a variable outside functions can be accessed broadly. Beginners use it to share simple constants or settings when appropriate. Changing globals from many places makes beginner programs harder to debug.

title: Function docstring basic
content: Function docstring basic in Python is a string placed near the start of a function to describe its purpose. Beginners use it to document inputs, outputs, and behavior for readers. A short docstring is better than no explanation for reusable functions.

title: Small function decomposition
content: Small function decomposition in Python means breaking a larger task into smaller named functions. Beginners use it to make code easier to test, read, and repair. Each function should have one clear job when possible.

title: Function returning boolean
content: Function returning boolean in Python returns True or False to answer a yes-or-no question. Beginners use it to build helpers such as is_prime or is_valid_name. The function name should make the boolean meaning clear.

title: Helper function basic
content: Helper function basic in Python is a smaller function used by another part of the program. Beginners use it to avoid repeating logic and keep main code cleaner. It should still have a clear name and clear inputs.

title: Lambda beginner caution
content: Lambda beginner caution in Python creates a small anonymous function expression. Beginners use it to use short one-line behavior in places that expect a function. Beginners should prefer def when the logic needs a clear name or multiple steps.

title: Function side effect basic
content: Function side effect basic in Python is a change a function makes outside returning a value, such as printing or modifying a list. Beginners use it to understand why a function affects program state. Side effects are useful but should be intentional and easy to see.

title: Pure function beginner idea
content: Pure function beginner idea in Python returns a result based only on its inputs and avoids changing outside state. Beginners use it to make code easier to test and reason about. Not every function must be pure, but pure helpers are beginner-friendly.
aliases: Pure function beginner idea là gì

title: Main function pattern
content: Main function pattern in Python puts the main program flow inside a function often named main. Beginners use it to organize scripts and separate setup from helper functions. It is common in larger beginner programs but not required for every tiny exercise.

title: Testing function with inputs
content: Testing function with inputs in Python means calling a function with known values and checking the result. Beginners use it to confirm behavior before using the function in a bigger program. Testing edge cases helps catch mistakes early.
aliases: Testing function with inputs là gì

title: Function type expectation
content: Function type expectation in Python means a function often expects certain kinds of inputs. Beginners use it to avoid TypeError or wrong results from invalid arguments. Clear names, checks, or annotations can help communicate expectations.

title: Optional parameter with None
content: Optional parameter with None in Python uses None as a default to mean no value was provided. Beginners use it to avoid mutable default traps and represent missing optional data. The function can then create a fresh value inside when needed.
aliases: Optional parameter with None là gì

title: Refactor repeated code into function
content: Refactor repeated code into function in Python means moving repeated logic into a reusable function. Beginners use it to reduce copy-paste errors and make changes in one place. Beginners should look for repeated blocks that do the same job.
aliases: Refactor repeated code into function là gì

title: List indexing is zero based
content: List indexing is zero based in Python means the first item of a list is at index 0. Beginners use it to access a specific item by position. This is a common source of off-by-one mistakes for beginners.
aliases: List indexing is zero based là gì

title: Negative indexing in Python
content: Negative indexing in Python in Python uses negative numbers to count from the end of a sequence. Beginners use it to get the last item with index -1. It is convenient, but beginners should still understand normal positive indexes.
aliases: Negative indexing in Python là gì

title: List slicing basic
content: List slicing basic in Python creates a smaller sequence from a start, stop, and optional step. Beginners use it to copy part of a list or string. The stop index is excluded, like range.

title: List append basic
content: List append basic in Python adds one item to the end of an existing list. Beginners use it to collect results during a loop. Append changes the list in place and does not return the updated list.

title: List remove basic
content: List remove basic in Python deletes the first matching value from a list. Beginners use it to remove a known item when it exists. It raises an error if the value is not found.

title: List pop basic
content: List pop basic in Python removes and returns an item, usually the last one by default. Beginners use it to use a list like a simple stack. Using pop on an empty list causes an error.

title: List iteration basic
content: List iteration basic in Python means looping over each item in a list. Beginners use it to process many values with the same code. Direct item iteration is often clearer than indexing.

title: List comprehension basic
content: List comprehension basic in Python builds a new list from an expression and a loop in one line. Beginners use it to create transformed or filtered lists compactly. Very complex comprehensions can be harder to read than normal loops.

title: Nested list as matrix
content: Nested list as matrix in Python uses a list of lists to represent rows and columns. Beginners use it to store simple table or matrix data. Beginners should track row index and column index carefully.
aliases: Nested list as matrix là gì

title: Tuple basic use
content: Tuple basic use in Python stores an ordered group of values that should not change. Beginners use it to represent fixed pairs like coordinates or multiple return values. Use a list instead when items need frequent editing.

title: Tuple unpacking basic
content: Tuple unpacking basic in Python assigns multiple values from a tuple or sequence to multiple names. Beginners use it to read pairs such as x, y or key, value clearly. The number of target names must match the number of values.

title: Dictionary key value basic
content: Dictionary key value basic in Python stores values by keys instead of numeric positions. Beginners use it to look up information such as a student's score by name. Keys should be unique and usually immutable.
aliases: Dictionary key value basic là gì

title: Dictionary lookup basic
content: Dictionary lookup basic in Python gets a value by using its key. Beginners use it to retrieve data quickly when the key is known. Looking up a missing key directly causes KeyError.

title: Dictionary get method
content: Dictionary get method in Python returns a value for a key or a default when the key is missing. Beginners use it to avoid KeyError in simple cases. It is helpful for counting and optional settings.

title: Dictionary add and update
content: Dictionary add and update in Python assigns a value to a key, creating or replacing the entry. Beginners use it to store new facts or change existing data. Beginners should know that assigning the same key overwrites the old value.
aliases: Dictionary add and update là gì

title: Dictionary iteration basic
content: Dictionary iteration basic in Python can loop over keys, values, or key-value pairs. Beginners use it to process records stored by names or identifiers. The items method is useful when both key and value are needed.

title: Set uniqueness basic
content: Set uniqueness basic in Python keeps only unique values and removes duplicates. Beginners use it to find distinct items such as unique words or IDs. Sets are not designed to preserve a meaningful order.

title: Set membership check
content: Set membership check in Python is often efficient for checking whether a value exists. Beginners use it to speed up repeated membership tests. A set is a good choice when uniqueness and lookup matter more than order.

title: Set add and remove
content: Set add and remove in Python changes the contents of a set by inserting or deleting values. Beginners use it to maintain a collection of unique items. Remove can error if the value is missing, while discard avoids that error.
